{{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}Edited by Suzuki Yaichirō, “Current 神社 Laws and Regulations,” Suzuki Yaichirō, 1908, p. 150. | Founded = Unknown{{ill|lt=Kakegawa City Hall|Q109594222|WD=Q109594222|lt=Kakegawa City|1=Kakegawa City Office}} “Kakegawa City Plan for the Maintenance and Improvement of Historic Landscape,” January 2018, p. 79. | Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | Alternate name = | Pilgrimage designation = | Annual festival = October{{ill|lt=Kakegawa City Hall|Q109594222|WD=Q109594222|lt=Kakegawa City|1=Kakegawa City Office}} “Kakegawa City Plan for the Maintenance and Improvement of Historic Landscape,” January 2018, p. 80. | Rituals = Gion Festival | Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Toshi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Toshi Shrine''' (としじんじゃ, {{Lang-en|Toshi Jinja}}) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kakegawa City|Q823988|WD=Q823988|1=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. Under the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ancient shrine ranking system|1=UNKNOWN}}, its {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} is {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}. Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, its rank is {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Overview === It is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Shimomata-Minami, {{ill|lt=Kakegawa City|Q823988|WD=Q823988|1=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}“Toshi Shrine,” “[http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409002 Toshi Shrine - Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines]” {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines|Q134956727|WD=Q134956727|1=Shizuoka Prefectural Shrine Association}}.. It enshrines two deities, {{ill|lt=Ōtoshi-no-Kami|Q3028663|WD=Q3028663|1=Toshigami}} and {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|1=Ukanomitama}}, and counts as its {{ill|lt=ujiko|Q125562175|WD=Q125562175|1=Ujiko}} the residents of the Shimomata-chō Ward, Chūō 2-chōme Ward, Jūkyūshu Ward, Odaka-chō Ward, Chūō 3-chōme Ward, and Chūō-Takamachi Ward, which are districts of Kakegawa City. It is identified with the “Toshi 神社” listed as a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}{{ill|lt=Atsuo Masamune|Q3297160|WD=Q3297160|1=Masamune Atsuo}} (ed.), “Engishiki,” vol. 2, Nihon Koten Zenshū Kankōkai, 1929, p. 128.. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it held joint festivals together with {{ill|lt=Ryūbi Shrine|Q11679232|WD=Q11679232|1=Tatsuo Shrine}} and {{ill|lt=Shinmei Shrine (Nito, Kakegawa)|Q118146837|WD=Q118146837|lt=Shinmei Shrine|1=Shinmei-gū}}{{ill|lt=Kakegawa City Hall|Q109594222|WD=Q109594222|lt=Kakegawa City|1=Kakegawa City Office}} “Kakegawa City Plan for the Maintenance and Improvement of Historic Landscape,” January 2018, p. 77., which is known as the origin of today’s {{ill|lt=Kakegawa Festival|Q11498235|WD=Q11498235|1=Kakegawa Matsuri}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Ōtoshi-no-Kami|Q3028663|WD=Q3028663|1=Toshigami}} * {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|1=Ukanomitama}} === History === The date of its founding is unknown. However, since “Toshi 神社” is listed as a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (minor shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|lt=minor shrine|1=Shikinai Shosha}} of {{ill|lt=Sano District, Tōtōmi Province|Q11385111|WD=Q11385111|1=Saya district}} in volume 9 of the “{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}},” which was compiled in 927 ({{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|1=Enchō}} 5){{ill|lt=Sano District, Shizuoka|Q11385111|WD=Q11385111|1=Saya district}} was merged with {{ill|lt=Jōtō District|Q11426221|WD=Q11426221|1=Kitō district}} to form {{ill|lt=Ogasa District|Q7079825|WD=Q7079825|1=Ogasa District, Shizuoka}} in 1896., it is presumed that the shrine was founded prior to 927. The “Engishiki” is a legal code of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, compiled by {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Tokihira|Q2994735|WD=Q2994735|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}} and {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Tadahira|Q1397601|WD=Q1397601|1=Fujiwara no Tadahira}} under the order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Daigo|Q329753|WD=Q329753|1=Emperor Daigo}}. The lists of shrines recorded in volumes 9 and 10 are known as the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, and the shrines listed therein were called {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}. In the Edo period, it was generously protected by the {{ill|lt=Kakegawa Domain|Q3034456|WD=Q3034456|1=Kakegawa Domain}} and revered by the people as a guardian shrine of the western part of {{ill|lt=Kakegawa-juku|Q3070229|WD=Q3070229|1=Kakegawa-juku}} in Sano District{{ill|lt=Kakegawa-juku|Q3070229|WD=Q3070229|1=Kakegawa-juku}} and parts of Shimomata-chō, Jūkyūshu-chō, Nitō Village, Ōike Village, and Kuzukawa Village were merged in 1889 to establish {{ill|lt=Kakegawa Town|Q823988|WD=Q823988|lt=Kakegawa|1=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}}.. In 1743 (lunar calendar, {{ill|lt=Kanpō|Q1145798|WD=Q1145798|1=Kanpō}} 3, 2nd month, 21st day), {{ill|lt=Ogasawara Nagayasu|Q8515344|WD=Q8515344|1=Ogasawara Nagayuki}}, heir to the domain lord, paid a visit, and a record remains stating that he donated a pair of heishi (sake bottles) and one bu of gold on that occasion. A ridge tag also remains indicating that the main shrine was rebuilt in 1644 (lunar calendar, {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 4, 9th month). After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. Although records do not clearly indicate when the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} was roofed with tiles, the buildings were rebuilt in 1915 ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 4). In September 1926 (Taishō 15), the {{ill|lt=kagura hall|Q94498|WD=Q94498|1=Kagura-den}} was rebuilt. === Examination regarding its status as a Shikinaisha === === Engishiki === [[File:Engishiki cropped Engishiki Vol 2 Flame 69.jpg|thumb|200px|The section in volume 9 of the “{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}” included in the Nihon Koten Zenshū that mentions “Toshi 神社”]] The shrine’s origin record clearly states that it is a “Shikinaisha”, and Toshi Shrine is regarded as the Shikinaisha listed in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. However, since the Engishiki dates back to the Heian period, many Shikinaisha lost identifiable descendants over time. As a result, scholars of National Learning conducted long-term research to identify their successors, and the successor of the “Toshi 神社” has been debated repeatedly. Regarding its reading, volume 9 of the Engishiki in the Nihon Koten Zenshū gives furigana reading “Toshino” for “Toshi”. === Jinmyōchō kōshō === [[File:Ban Nobutomo Zenshu cropped Ban Nobutomo Zenshu Vol 1 Frame 121.jpg|thumb|200px|A section in “神meichō Kōshō” included in the Ban Nobutomo Zenshū that discusses “Toshi 神社”{{ill|lt=Kenkichi Ichishima|Q11480125|WD=Q11480125|1=Kenkichi Ichishima}} (ed.), “Ban Nobutomo Zenshū,” vol. 1, {{ill|lt=Kokusho Kankōkai (1905–1922)|Q28686029|WD=Q28686029|1=Kokusho Kankōkai (1905–1922)}}, 1907, p. 226.]] The Edo-period scholar {{ill|lt=Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747|WD=Q4346747|1=Ban Nobutomo}} researched ancient shrines and authored “{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō (Dai)|Q11588823|WD=Q11588823|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|1=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dodai}}.” In it, he also discusses the “Toshi 神社” of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, introducing the theory that “west of Kakegawa-juku there is a place called Jūkyūshu, where there is Toshi Daimyōjin; this must be it”. Regarding its reading, he gives furigana “Tokami” for “Toshi”. === Jinja Kakuroku === [[File:Jinja Kakuroku cropped Jinja Kakuroku Part Jo Frame 439.jpg|thumb|200px|A section in the upper volume of “{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708|lt=神社 Kakuroku|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}” discussing “Toshi 神社”{{ill|lt=Suzuka Tsugutane|Q6536446|WD=Q6536446|lt=Nakatomi no Asomi Tsugutane|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}} (sel.), “Tōtōmi Province,” authored by Suzuka Yoshikujira; edited by {{ill|lt=Yorikuni Inoue|Q6535302|WD=Q6535302|1=Inoue Yorikuni}} and {{ill|lt=Ariyoshi Saeki|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}}, “神社 Kakuroku,” upper volume, {{ill|lt=Kōten Kōkyūjo|Q11580997|WD=Q11580997|1=Koten Kokyusho}}, 1902, p. 857.]] On the other hand, the Edo-period Shinto priest {{ill|lt=Suzuka Tsugutane|Q6536446|WD=Q6536446|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}} also researched ancient shrines and authored the “{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}.” Therein, he states regarding the “Toshi 神社” of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō that the details of the enshrined deity and location were already unknown. He then introduces the same theory that “west of Kakegawa-juku there is a place called Jūkyūshu, where there is Toshi Daimyōjin; this must be it”. Regarding its reading, he states that “Toshi should be read as ‘To’”. === Tokusen Shimmeichō === In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, research into ancient shrines also progressed within the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Religion|Q4294653|WD=Q4294653|1=Ministry of Religion (Japan)}}, one of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=central government ministries|1=UNKNOWN}}, and the results were published as the “{{ill|lt=Tokusen Shimmeichō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092|1=Tokusen Jinmyōchō}}.” In it, the location of the “Toshi 神社” of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō is clearly identified as “Kamegaya aza, Shimomata Village ({{ill|lt=Ogasa District|Q7079825|WD=Q7079825|1=Ogasa District, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=Kakegawa Town|Q823988|WD=Q823988|lt=Kakegawa|1=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}}{{ill|lt=ōaza|Q424857|WD=Q424857|1=ōaza}} Shimomata)”, making a definitive identification. Regarding its reading, furigana “Toshikami-no” is given for “Toshi”. === Shrine precincts === Within the precincts stand a haiden with a heiden attached, covering the {{ill|lt=main hall|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} of {{ill|lt=kokerabuki|Q199388|WD=Q199388|1=Wood shingle}} {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style, as well as a kagura hall. Behind the precincts is Toshigami Pond. Adjacent to the precincts is Toshigami Park“Toshigami Park,” “[https://www.city.kakegawa.shizuoka.jp/gyosei/docs/11065.html Toshigami Park - Kakegawa City]” {{ill|lt=Kakegawa City Hall|Q109594222|WD=Q109594222|1=Kakegawa City Office}}, November 21, 2014.. === Festivals and annual events === Known festivals include the Gion Festival and the Autumn Festival. Prior to the Meiji period, the Gion Festival was held on June 15, and the Autumn Festival on September 14. From 1920 (Taishō 9), the Gion and Autumn festivals have been held jointly in October. Toshi Shrine also conducts the Kakegawa Festival as a joint festival of seven shrines together with Ryūbi Shrine, Shinmei Shrine, Ikebe Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, Tsushima Shrine, and Kifune Shrine. Originally, the Kakegawa Festival originated from festivals jointly conducted by Toshi Shrine with Ryūbi Shrine and Shinmei Shrine, and is said to have been held since the mid-Edo period. In the Edo period, it was rare nationwide for multiple shrines to jointly conduct a festival{{ill|lt=Kakegawa City Hall|Q109594222|WD=Q109594222|lt=Kakegawa City|1=Kakegawa City Office}} “Kakegawa City Plan for the Maintenance and Improvement of Historic Landscape,” January 2018, p. 84.. At the time, Kakegawa-juku was located within the sōguruwa (overall enclosure) of Kakegawa Castle. As a result, unlike other castle towns, the samurai quarter and the post town were integrated, and the parish areas of the three shrines—Ryūbi Shrine, Shinmei Shrine, and Toshi Shrine—were intermingled, which formed the background for this arrangement. {{See also|Kakegawa Festival}} === Parishioner areas === * Shimomata-chō Ward, {{ill|lt=Kakegawa City|Q823988|WD=Q823988|1=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} * Chūō 2-chōme Ward, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture * Jūkyūshu Ward, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture * Odaka-chō Ward, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture * Chūō 3-chōme Ward, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture * Chūō-Takamachi Ward, Kakegawa City, Shizuoka Prefecture === Chronology === * 1644 – Main shrine rebuilt. * 1915 – Buildings rebuilt. * 1926 – {{ill|lt=Kagura hall|Q94498|WD=Q94498|1=Kagura-den}} rebuilt. === Notes === {{Footnote help}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="†"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} === Related people === * {{ill|lt=Ogasawara Nagayasu|Q8515344|WD=Q8515344|1=Ogasawara Nagayuki}} * {{ill|lt=Suzuka Tsugutane|Q6536446|WD=Q6536446|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}} * {{ill|lt=Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747|WD=Q4346747|1=Ban Nobutomo}} === Related topics === * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province|Q11642130|WD=Q11642130|1=List of Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province}} * {{ill|lt=Gion faith|Q11588425|WD=Q11588425|1=Gion Faith}} * {{ill|lt=Kakegawa Festival|Q11498235|WD=Q11498235|1=Kakegawa Matsuri}} === External links === * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4409002 Toshi Shrine - Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines] – A page introducing Toshi Shrine by the {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines|Q134956727|WD=Q134956727|1=Shizuoka Prefectural Shrine Association}} * [https://www.kakegawa-kankou.com/matsuri/ The grand festival held once every three years: “Kakegawa Grand Festival”] – Official website of the Kakegawa Tourism Association introducing the Kakegawa Grand Festival {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Shizuoka Prefecture}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Shinto sidebar}} {{Authority control}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Toshi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Toshi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Toshi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha in Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kakegawa, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:遠江国の式内社]]
